rule: accept と except の区別
rule_en: Accept vs. Except
pattern: accept = 動詞（受け取る・同意する）/ except = 前置詞（〜を除いて）
correct_example: He accepted their praise graciously. / Everyone went to the game except Alyson.

common_errors.wrong: Everyone went to the game accept Alyson.
common_errors.why: acceptは動詞であり前置詞として使えない
exceptは前置詞であり動詞として使う場合は意味が異なる

key_signal: 後に名詞が続いて「〜を除いて」の意味ならexcept、「受け取る・同意する」の動詞ならaccept
sources: Purdue OWL: Spelling: Common Words that Sound Alike

rule: affect と effect の区別（RAVEN）
rule_en: Affect vs. Effect
pattern: affect = 動詞（影響する）/ effect = 名詞（結果・影響）/ effect = 動詞（もたらす）は例外
correct_example: Will lack of sleep affect your game? / Will lack of sleep have an effect on your game?

common_errors.wrong: Will lack of sleep effect your game?
common_errors.why: effectを動詞として使う場合は「もたらす」の意味で、「影響する」ではない

common_errors.wrong: The affect of stress is significant.
common_errors.why: affectは動詞なので名詞位置には使えない

key_signal: RAVEN: Remember Affect is a Verb and Effect is a Noun
sources: Purdue OWL: Spelling: Common Words that Sound Alike

rule: advise と advice の区別
rule_en: Advise vs. Advice
pattern: advise = 動詞（勧める・助言する）/ advice = 名詞（助言・意見）
correct_example: I advise you to be cautious. / I'd like to ask for your advice on this matter.

common_errors.why: adviceは名詞なので動詞位置には使えない
advise は動詞であり名詞ではない。名詞形は advice

key_signal: 動詞ならadvise（末尾-ise）、名詞ならadvice（末尾-ice）
sources: Purdue OWL: Spelling: Common Words that Sound Alike

rule: conscious と conscience の区別
rule_en: Conscious vs. Conscience
pattern: conscious = 形容詞（意識がある・気づいている）/ conscience = 名詞（良心）
correct_example: Despite a head injury, the patient remained conscious. / Chris wouldn't cheat because his conscience wouldn't let him.

common_errors.wrong: His conscience was still conscious after surgery.
common_errors.why: conscious（形容詞）とconscience（名詞）の品詞と意味の混同
consciousは形容詞であり名詞として使えない。名詞はconscience

key_signal: 「良心」の意味ならconscience（名詞）、「意識がある」ならconscious（形容詞）
sources: Purdue OWL: Spelling: Common Words that Sound Alike

rule: 数字を単語で書く基準（1〜2語）
rule_en: Writing Numbers: Spell out one or two word numbers
pattern: 1〜2語で表せる数字はスペルアウト、3語以上の数字は数字（アラビア数字）で書く
correct_example: over two pounds / six million dollars / after 126 days / only $31.50

common_errors.why: thirty-oneは2語なのでスペルアウトが原則
126のように3語以上になる場合は数字で表す

key_signal: その数字を英語で書いたとき何語になるか確認し、1〜2語ならスペルアウト

rule: 文頭の数字は必ずスペルアウト
rule_en: Write out numbers beginning sentences
pattern: 文の先頭に数字を置く場合は必ずスペルアウト（アラビア数字は不可）
correct_example: Six percent of the group failed.

common_errors.why: 文頭にアラビア数字・記号を置くことはできない

key_signal: 文の最初の単語が数字になる場合は必ずスペルアウトする

rule: 同一文中の数字の一貫性
rule_en: Numbers in series should be consistent
pattern: 同一文または同一文脈内の数字は、アラビア数字かスペルアウトかを統一する
correct_example: two apples, six oranges, and three bananas

common_errors.why: 同じ文脈で数字の表記形式が混在している

key_signal: シリーズや統計の数字は表記スタイルを揃える

rule: 形容詞の基本：名詞を修飾する
rule_en: Adjectives modify nouns
pattern: 形容詞は名詞を修飾し、What kind? / Which? / How many? という問いに答える
correct_example: I ate an enormous meal. / The tall girl is riding her bike. / Fifteen students passed the exam.

common_errors.why: quickは形容詞なので動詞(ate)を修飾できない。副詞quicklyを使う必要がある
quietlyは副詞なので名詞(man)を修飾できない。形容詞quietを使う

key_signal: 修飾対象が名詞なら形容詞、動詞・形容詞・副詞なら副詞を使う

rule: 副詞の基本：動詞・形容詞・副詞を修飾する
rule_en: Adverbs modify verbs, adjectives, and other adverbs
pattern: 副詞は動詞・形容詞・他の副詞を修飾する。多くの副詞は形容詞に-lyを付けて形成される
correct_example: Richard talks carelessly. / Priya was extremely happy.

common_errors.why: happilyは副詞なので名詞(dog)を修飾できない。形容詞happyを使う
quickは形容詞。動詞を修飾するには副詞quicklyが必要

key_signal: 動詞を修飾しているかどうかを確認し、副詞が必要かチェックする

rule: be動詞・感覚動詞の後は形容詞
rule_en: Adjective follows be-verbs and sense verbs
pattern: be動詞および feel/taste/smell/sound/look/appear/seem の後に来て名詞を修飾するのは形容詞
correct_example: I was nervous. / Sharon's cough sounds bad. / Castor oil tastes awful. / The ocean air smells fresh.

common_errors.why: badlyは副詞で「においをかぐのが下手」という意味になる。外見・感覚を表すsmellの後は形容詞badが正しい
seemの後は形容詞が来るのでunhappyが正しい

key_signal: feel/taste/smell/sound/look/appear/seem/be の後に主語の状態を述べるなら形容詞

rule: bad と badly の使い分け
rule_en: Bad or Badly?
pattern: 感覚動詞(feel/smell等)の後は形容詞bad。動作動詞の後は副詞badly
correct_example: I feel bad. (体調が悪い) / The dog smells bad. (犬が臭い) / She plays football badly. (下手にプレーする)

common_errors.why: 「触覚機能が低下している」という意味になり、「気分が悪い」の意味にならない
「犬のにおいをかぐ能力が低い」という意味になる

key_signal: feelの後に体調・感情を述べるなら形容詞bad。動作の質を述べるなら副詞badly

rule: good と well の使い分け
rule_en: Good or Well?
pattern: goodは形容詞（名詞・感覚動詞後）、wellは副詞（動作動詞後）
correct_example: My mother looks good. / I feel really good today. / I did well on my exam. / She plays tennis well.

common_errors.wrong: She did good on her exam.
common_errors.why: doは動作動詞なので副詞wellが必要

common_errors.wrong: I feel well today. (健康の意味で)
common_errors.why: 感覚動詞feelの後に健康・幸福を表す場合は形容詞goodが自然

key_signal: 動作動詞（do/play等）の後はwell、感覚動詞・be動詞の後はgood

rule: sure と surely の使い分け
rule_en: Sure or Surely?
pattern: sureは形容詞（名詞・主語を修飾）、surelyは副詞（動詞や文全体を修飾）
correct_example: He is sure about his answer. / The Senator spoke out surely. / Surely, you're joking.

common_errors.why: 動詞answeredを修飾するには副詞surelyが必要
be動詞の後の述語は形容詞sureが正しい

key_signal: 主語の状態を説明するならsure（形容詞）、動詞や文全体を修飾するならsurely（副詞）

rule: 可算名詞と不可算名詞：much vs. many
rule_en: Much modifies uncountable nouns; Many modifies countable nouns
pattern: much + 不可算名詞 / many + 可算名詞
correct_example: They have so much money in the bank. / Many Americans travel to Europe.

common_errors.why: ringsは可算名詞（複数形）なのでmanyが正しい
restは不可算名詞なのでmuchが正しい

key_signal: 名詞に数字をつけられるか試す。つけられれば可算名詞でmany、つけられなければ不可算名詞でmuch
sources: Purdue OWL: How to Use Adjectives and Adverbs

rule: 不定冠詞 a vs. an：音に基づく選択
rule_en: A versus An: based on phonetic quality
pattern: 次の語が母音の音で始まるならan、子音の音で始まるならa（綴りではなく音で判断）
correct_example: an apricot / an egg / an Indian / a cat / a dog / an honorable peace / a union / a unicorn

common_errors.why: honestの頭のhは発音されないため母音音で始まり、anが正しい
unionの頭はyの音（子音）なのでaが正しい
oneの頭はwの音（子音）なのでaが正しい

key_signal: 綴りではなく発音で判断する。サイレントhの語はan、u/oが子音音で始まる語はa
sources: Purdue OWL: Articles: A versus An

rule: 定冠詞 the と不定冠詞 a/an の使い分け
rule_en: Definite article the vs. indefinite article a/an
pattern: the = 特定・既知のもの（定冠詞）/ a/an = 不特定・任意のもの（不定冠詞）
correct_example: Let's read the book. (特定の本) / Let's read a book. (どれかの本) / I just saw the most popular movie of the year.

common_errors.why: 特定の警官を指しているのでなければaが正しい

common_errors.wrong: I want to see a most popular movie.
common_errors.why: 最上級で特定された1つのものにはtheが必要

key_signal: 読者・話し手が同じものを指していると分かる（特定）ならthe、分からない（不特定）ならa/an

rule: 同格語（アポジティブ）の基本
rule_en: Appositives: noun set beside another noun to explain or identify it
pattern: 名詞または代名詞の直後（または直前）に置かれ、その名詞を説明・同定する名詞句
correct_example: Your friend Bill is in trouble. / My brother's car, a sporty red convertible with bucket seats, is the envy of my friends.

common_errors.wrong: My brother's car a sporty red convertible is the envy of my friends.
common_errors.why: 非制限的同格語（なくても意味が通る）にはコンマが必要

common_errors.wrong: The, president, John Kennedy, was known for his speeches.
common_errors.why: 制限的同格語（ないと誰か分からない）にはコンマ不要

key_signal: 同格語なしで文の意味が完結するならコンマあり（非制限）、なしでは不明確ならコンマなし（制限）

rule: 同格語のコンマ：制限的vs非制限的
rule_en: Punctuation of appositives: restrictive vs. non-restrictive
pattern: 同格語が文の意味に必須（制限的）→コンマなし / 同格語がなくても意味が通る（非制限的）→コンマあり
correct_example: The popular US president John Kennedy was known for his speeches. (制限的) / John Kennedy, the popular US president, was known for his speeches. (非制限的)

common_errors.wrong: The popular US president, John Kennedy, was known for his speeches.
common_errors.why: 「どの大統領か」を特定する情報なのでコンマは不要（制限的）

common_errors.wrong: John Kennedy the popular US president was known for his speeches.
common_errors.why: John Kennedyだけで特定できるためコンマが必要（非制限的）

key_signal: 同格語を取り除いて文の意味が通るか・誰のことか分かるかを確認する

rule: 前置詞 on/at/in：時間の使い分け
rule_en: Prepositions for Time: on, at, in
pattern: on + 曜日 / at + 時刻・noon・night・midnight / in + 午前午後・月・年・季節
correct_example: I will see you on Monday. / My plane leaves at noon. / He likes to read in the afternoon. / The book was published in 1999. / The flowers will bloom in spring.

common_errors.wrong: I will see you in Monday.
common_errors.why: 曜日にはonを使う
時刻にはatを使う

common_errors.wrong: The days are long on August.
common_errors.why: 月にはinを使う

key_signal: 曜日→on、時刻・noon/night→at、月・年・季節・時間帯→in

rule: 前置詞 at/on/in：場所の使い分け
rule_en: Prepositions of Location: at (point), on (surface), in (area/volume)
pattern: at = 点（一般的な場所・地点）/ on = 面（表面）/ in = 面積・体積（内部に含まれる）
correct_example: My car is at the house. / There is a new roof on the house. / The house is in Tippecanoe county. / There is a wasp in the house.

common_errors.wrong: She is in the bus stop.
common_errors.why: バス停は点なのでatが正しい

common_errors.wrong: There is a book at the table.
common_errors.why: 表面に置かれているのでonが正しい
国・地域の内部を示すにはinが正しい

key_signal: 点的な場所→at、表面→on、内部・区域→in
sources: Purdue OWL: Prepositions of Location: At, In, On

rule: 前置詞 since/for/by/during：継続時間の表現
rule_en: Prepositions for Extended Time: since, for, by, during, within
pattern: since + 起点 / for + 期間 / during + 期間名詞 / within + 期限
correct_example: She has been gone since yesterday. / I'm going to Paris for two weeks. / I watch TV during the evening. / We must finish the project within a year.

common_errors.why: 起点（年・日付）にはsinceを使う

common_errors.wrong: She has been gone during three hours.
common_errors.why: 数値で表す期間にはforを使う。duringは名詞期間に使う

key_signal: 起点（since）・数値期間（for）・名詞期間（during）・上限（within）で区別する

rule: 方向の前置詞 to/onto/into の使い分け
rule_en: Prepositions of Direction: to, onto, into
pattern: to = 目標への方向 / onto = 表面への移動 / into = 内部への移動
correct_example: We flew from New York to Paris. / The frog jumped onto the lilypad. / The milk went into the glass.

common_errors.wrong: The cat jumped in the table.
common_errors.why: 表面への移動なのでonto（またはon to）が正しい

common_errors.wrong: She walked to the room and into.
common_errors.why: intoは移動動詞と共に使い内部への方向を示す必要がある

key_signal: 移動先が表面ならonto、内部ならinto、目標・目的地ならto
sources: Purdue OWL: Prepositions of Direction: To, On (to), In (to)

rule: 空間的前置詞の一覧（above/below/between等）
rule_en: Prepositions of Spatial Relationship
pattern: above（上方）/below（下方）/between（2つの間）/among（3つ以上の間）/beside（隣）/behind（後ろ）/in front of（前）/near（近く）/through（通り抜けて）/toward（方向）
correct_example: Write your name above the line. / She is between two trees. / He is among the trees. / She went through the door.

common_errors.wrong: He is between the trees. (3本以上)
common_errors.why: 3つ以上の間にはamongを使う
2つの間にはbetweenを使う

key_signal: 2者の間→between、3者以上の間→among
sources: Purdue OWL: Prepositions of Spatial Relationship

rule: 代名詞の数の一致
rule_en: Pronouns must agree in number with their antecedents
pattern: 代名詞は先行詞（antecedent）と数（単数・複数）が一致しなければならない
correct_example: The girls brought their umbrella.

common_errors.why: 先行詞the girls（複数）に対し単数代名詞herを使っている

key_signal: 先行詞が複数ならtheir/them、単数ならhis/her/its/himを使う

rule: andで結ばれた複数の主語には複数形の動詞を使う
rule_en: When a subject is composed of two or more nouns/pronouns connected by 'and', use a plural verb.
pattern: [Noun1] and [Noun2] + plural verb
correct_example: She and her friends are at the fair.
common_errors: She and her friends is at the fair.
key_signal: and

rule: orまたはnorで結ばれた複数の単数名詞・代名詞には単数形の動詞を使う
rule_en: When two or more singular nouns/pronouns are connected by 'or' or 'nor', use a singular verb.
pattern: [Singular Noun1] or/nor [Singular Noun2] + singular verb
correct_example: The book or the pen is in the drawer.
common_errors: The book or the pen are in the drawer.
key_signal: or / nor

rule: or/norで単数・複数の名詞が結ばれる場合、動詞は動詞に近い方の主語に一致させる
rule_en: When a compound subject contains both a singular and a plural noun joined by 'or'/'nor', the verb agrees with the part of the subject nearer to the verb.
pattern: [Singular] or [Plural] + plural verb / [Plural] or [Singular] + singular verb
correct_example: The boy or his friends run every day. / His friends or the boy runs every day.
common_errors: The boy or his friends runs every day.
key_signal: or / nor (mixed number subjects)

rule: doesn'tは単数主語、don'tは複数主語に使う（I・youはdon't）
rule_en: 'Doesn't' is used only with a singular subject; 'don't' is used only with a plural subject. Exception: 'I' and 'you' use 'don't'.
pattern: He/She/It + doesn't | They/We/I/You + don't
correct_example: He doesn't like it. / They don't like it.
common_errors: He don't like it.
They doesn't like it.
key_signal: doesn't / don't

rule: 主語と動詞の間に挿入句があっても、動詞は主語に一致させる
rule_en: Do not be misled by a phrase between the subject and the verb; the verb agrees with the subject, not with a noun in the intervening phrase.
pattern: [Subject] + [intervening phrase] + verb agreeing with subject
correct_example: One of the boxes is open. / The team captain, as well as his players, is anxious.
common_errors: One of the boxes are open.
The team captain, as well as his players, are anxious.
key_signal: one of, as well as, including

rule: each, everyone, everybodyなどの不定代名詞は単数扱いで単数動詞を使う
rule_en: Words such as each, each one, either, neither, everyone, everybody, anybody, anyone, nobody, somebody, someone, and no one are singular and require a singular verb.
pattern: each/everyone/nobody/somebody + singular verb
correct_example: Everyone is here. / Nobody knows the answer.
common_errors: Everyone are here.
Nobody know the answer.
key_signal: each, everyone, everybody, anyone, nobody, somebody, someone

rule: 単数のtheyは性別を特定しない個人または非二元的な性自認を持つ人を指す代名詞として使える
rule_en: Singular 'they' can be used to refer to an individual person whose gender is unknown, non-binary, or unspecified, and is officially recognized as correct by major style guides.
pattern: [Person of unspecified/non-binary gender] → they/them/their
correct_example: A student should bring their own pen. / Alex said they would arrive at noon.
common_errors: A student should bring his pen. (when gender is unknown)
Use of he as generic default
key_signal: unknown gender / non-binary person / gender-neutral context

rule: 関係代名詞は先行詞を修飾する従属節（関係節）を導く
rule_en: Relative pronouns (who, whom, whose, that, which) introduce relative clauses that modify a word, phrase, or idea in the main clause.
pattern: [Antecedent] + [relative pronoun] + [relative clause]
correct_example: The house that Jack built is large. / The professor, whom I respect, recently received tenure.
common_errors: The house which Jack built is large. (in restrictive clause, 'that' preferred)
Omitting the relative pronoun in subject position
key_signal: who, whom, whose, that, which

rule: 制限用法の関係節（定義節）はコンマで区切らず、情報を省略できない
rule_en: Restrictive (defining) relative clauses add essential information and are NOT separated from the main clause by a comma.
pattern: [Antecedent] + that/who/which [no comma] + [essential information]
correct_example: The book that she recommended is excellent.
common_errors: The book, that she recommended, is excellent.
key_signal: essential information / no comma

rule: 非制限用法の関係節（非定義節）はコンマで区切り、thatは使えない
rule_en: Non-defining (non-restrictive) relative clauses provide additional, non-essential information, ARE separated by commas, and cannot use 'that'.
pattern: [Antecedent], who/which/whose, [additional information]
correct_example: The writer, who lives in this mansion, has published his second novel.
common_errors: The writer, that lives in this mansion, has published his second novel.
Omitting the commas in non-defining clauses
key_signal: additional / non-essential information, commas

rule: 人を指す関係代名詞にはwhoまたはwhom、物・概念にはwhichを使う
rule_en: Use 'who' or 'whom' to refer to people; use 'which' to refer to things or concepts.
pattern: [Person] + who/whom | [Thing/Concept] + which
correct_example: The teacher who inspired me retired last year. / The car, which was red, sped past.
common_errors: The teacher which inspired me retired.
The car, who was red, sped past.
key_signal: person → who/whom; thing → which

rule: 節全体を先行詞とする非制限用法の関係節にはwhichを使う
rule_en: Use 'which' when referring to the previous clause as a whole in a non-defining clause.
pattern: [Main clause], which [refers to whole clause]
correct_example: My friend decided to get divorced, which upset me a lot.
common_errors: My friend decided to get divorced, that upset me a lot.
key_signal: referring to previous whole clause

rule: most, many, some, both, noneなどの後で人にはof whom、物にはof whichを使う
rule_en: After numbers and words like most, many, some, both, none: use 'of whom' for persons and 'of which' for things/concepts.
pattern: [Number/Quantifier] of whom/which
correct_example: I saw many people at the party, some of whom seemed familiar.
common_errors: some of which seemed familiar (when referring to people)
key_signal: most, many, some, both, none + of whom/of which

rule: コンマの有無によって定義節と非定義節の意味が変わる
rule_en: The presence or absence of a comma distinguishes defining from non-defining clauses and changes the meaning of the sentence.
pattern: Noun + that ... (no comma = defining) vs. Noun, who/which ... (comma = non-defining)
correct_example: They have two sons who are doctors. (more sons exist) / They have two sons, who are doctors. (only two sons)
common_errors: Ignoring the comma distinction, causing unintended meaning
key_signal: comma = non-defining / no comma = defining

rule: 書き言葉では、人はwho、物はwhichを優先し、thatの前にコンマを置かない
rule_en: In writing, prefer 'who' for people and 'which' for things over 'that'; never put a comma before 'that'.
pattern: In writing: people → who | things → which | no comma before that
correct_example: The student who passed the exam was rewarded. / The report, which was thorough, impressed the committee.
common_errors: The student that passed... (in formal writing)
The report, that was thorough...
key_signal: formal writing context

rule: 可算名詞は複数形になれるが、不可算名詞は原則として複数形にならない
rule_en: Count nouns can be expressed in plural form; noncount nouns usually cannot be expressed in plural form.
pattern: Count noun + -s/-es (plural) | Noncount noun (no plural)
correct_example: cats, students (count) / milk, water, air (noncount)
common_errors: moneys
waters (in general sense)
furnitures
key_signal: can be counted vs. cannot be counted

rule: muchは不可算名詞、manyは可算名詞を修飾する
rule_en: 'Much' modifies only uncountable nouns; 'many' modifies only countable nouns.
pattern: much + noncount noun | many + count noun
correct_example: We don't have much time. / Many Americans travel to Europe.
common_errors: many time
much students
key_signal: much / many

rule: littleは不可算名詞、fewは可算名詞を修飾する
rule_en: 'Little' modifies only uncountable nouns; 'few' modifies only countable nouns.
pattern: little + noncount noun | few + count noun
correct_example: He had little food in the house. / Few students like exams.
common_errors: few food
little students
key_signal: little / few

rule: some/anyは可算名詞・不可算名詞の両方に使える
rule_en: 'Some' and 'any' can be used with both countable and uncountable nouns.
pattern: some/any + count or noncount noun
correct_example: There is some water on the floor. / There are some students here. / Do you have any food?
common_errors: Using 'much' where 'any' is appropriate
key_signal: some / any

rule: 英語の動詞には現在形と過去形の2つの基本時制があり、それ以外は助動詞で表す
rule_en: Only two tenses are conveyed through the verb alone: present and past. Most other English tenses are marked by auxiliary verbs.
pattern: present: walk | past: walked | others: auxiliary + verb
correct_example: They walk. (present) / They walked. (past) / They will walk. (future, with auxiliary)
common_errors: Confusing simple tenses with perfect or progressive forms
key_signal: verb alone = only 2 tenses

rule: 現在完了形は過去に始まり現在まで続く動作・状態、または効果が今も残る完了した行為を表す
rule_en: The present perfect (have/has + past participle) designates action that began in the past but continues into the present, or whose effect still continues.
pattern: have/has + past participle
correct_example: Betty has taught for ten years. (still teaching) / He has lived here for many years.
common_errors: Betty taught for ten years. (implies she stopped)
Using simple past for ongoing situations
key_signal: has/have + past participle; for, since, ever, never

rule: 過去完了形は過去の別の出来事よりも前に完了した行為・状態を表す
rule_en: The past perfect (had + past participle) describes an action that began and ended in the past, before another past event.
pattern: had + past participle
correct_example: I had eaten dinner before he came. / Mike had promised to repair Joe's bike.
common_errors: I ate dinner before he came. (loses the sequence of past events)
key_signal: had + past participle; before, after, when (sequence of past events)

rule: 現在進行形は今進行中の動作または近い将来の予定を表す
rule_en: The present progressive (is/am/are + verb-ing) shows action currently in progress or planned for the near future.
pattern: is/am/are + verb-ing
correct_example: I am playing soccer now. / Next week they are going to Florida.
common_errors: I play soccer now. (misses the in-progress aspect)
key_signal: now, at the moment, this week

rule: 過去進行形は過去のある時点で進行中だった動作、または別の動作に割り込まれた動作を表す
rule_en: The past progressive (was/were + verb-ing) shows action that was in progress at a certain time in the past, or was interrupted by another action.
pattern: was/were + verb-ing
correct_example: They were climbing for twenty-seven days. / We were eating dinner when she told me.
common_errors: We ate dinner when she told me. (loses the in-progress aspect)
key_signal: when, while, at that time

rule: 助動詞（can, will, shall, may, could, would, should, might, must）は主語に関わらず形が変わらない
rule_en: Modal auxiliaries (can, will, shall, may, could, would, should, might, must) do not change form to agree with the subject.
pattern: [Subject] + modal + base verb (modal does not inflect)
correct_example: I can do it. / He can do it. / They can do it.
common_errors: He cans do it.
She musts go.
key_signal: can, will, shall, may, could, would, should, might, must

rule: could have / would have / must haveの後には過去分詞を置く（'of'は誤り）
rule_en: Modal perfect constructions use 'could have', 'would have', 'must have' + past participle. 'Could of', 'would of' are incorrect.
pattern: could/would/must + have + past participle
correct_example: I could have won the prize. / He must have bought the ticket already.
common_errors: I could of won the prize.
He must of bought the ticket.
key_signal: could have / would have / must have

rule: 動詞の時制を不必要に変えてはならない。同一時間軸の動作は同じ時制で統一する
rule_en: Do not shift from one tense to another unnecessarily if the time frame for each action or state is the same.
pattern: Maintain consistent tense throughout same time-frame narration
correct_example: The instructor explains the diagram to students who ask questions during the lecture.
common_errors: The instructor explains the diagram to students who asked questions during the lecture.
key_signal: same time frame = same tense

rule: 受動態は行為者が不明・不要なとき、またはトピックを前景化したいときに使う
rule_en: Use passive verbs when the actor is unknown or irrelevant, or when you wish to foreground a topic that is not the action or actor.
pattern: be + past participle (by [agent] — optional)
correct_example: Crimes were committed. / Penicillin was developed in 1928.
common_errors: Overusing passive voice when active is clearer
Using passive when the actor is important and known
key_signal: actor unknown / irrelevant; topic foregrounding

rule: 不規則動詞は過去形・過去分詞形が規則的な-ed形にならず、固有の変化をする
rule_en: Irregular verbs do not follow the standard -ed pattern for past and past participle forms; they take on alternative patterns.
pattern: Present / Past / Past Participle (no -ed rule)
correct_example: go/went/gone, eat/ate/eaten, begin/began/begun, drink/drank/drunk
common_errors: goed
eated
beginned
drinked
key_signal: memorization required; no -ed pattern

rule: 仮定法過去完了
rule_en: Past Unreal Conditional (Third Conditional)
pattern: If + S + had + pp, S + would have + pp
correct_example: If I had studied harder, I would have passed the exam.

common_errors.wrong: If I studied harder, I would have passed.
common_errors.why: 過去の事実に反する仮定にはhad+ppが必要

common_errors.wrong: If I had studied, I would pass.
common_errors.why: 帰結節もwould have+ppが必要

key_signal: 過去の事実に反する仮定 → If had pp, would have pp

rule: 仮定法過去
rule_en: Present Unreal Conditional (Second Conditional)
pattern: If + S + V(過去形), S + would + V(原形)
correct_example: If I were you, I would accept the offer.

common_errors.wrong: If I was you, I would accept.
common_errors.why: 仮定法ではwereを使う（格式）

common_errors.wrong: If I am you, I would accept.
common_errors.why: 現在の事実に反する仮定には過去形

key_signal: 現在の事実に反する仮定 → If V(過去形), would V

rule: 仮定法の倒置 (Had S pp)
rule_en: Inverted Conditional (Had + Subject + Past Participle)
pattern: Had + S + pp, S + would have + pp (= If S had pp)
correct_example: Had I known the truth, I would have acted differently.

common_errors.why: had + 過去分詞。原形は不可

common_errors.wrong: Had I known, I would act.
common_errors.why: 帰結節もwould have + ppが必要

key_signal: If省略→倒置: Had S pp / Were S to / Should S

rule: 否定語句の倒置
rule_en: Inversion with Negative Adverbials
pattern: 否定語句(Never/Hardly/Not only) + 倒置(助動詞+S+V)
correct_example: Not only does he speak English, but he also speaks French.

common_errors.why: 否定語句が文頭→倒置が必要
Hardlyが文頭→had he begun

key_signal: Never/Hardly/Seldom/Not only が文頭 → 倒置

rule: Hardly had S pp when ...
rule_en: Hardly...When / No sooner...Than
pattern: Hardly + had + S + pp + when + S + V(過去形)
correct_example: Hardly had he begun the presentation when the fire alarm went off.

common_errors.why: 倒置が必要 (had he begun)

common_errors.wrong: Hardly had he begun... than the alarm.
common_errors.why: hardly→when / no sooner→than

key_signal: Hardly/Scarcely + when, No sooner + than

rule: Were it not for（仮定法倒置）
rule_en: Were It Not For (Inverted Conditional)
pattern: Were it not for + N, S + would/could + V
correct_example: Were it not for his help, we could not have succeeded.

common_errors.why: forが必要

common_errors.wrong: Was it not for his help...
common_errors.why: 仮定法ではwere (wasは不可)

key_signal: Were it not for = If it were not for = But for

rule: suggest/recommend + that S + 原形
rule_en: Subjunctive Mood after Suggest/Recommend
pattern: suggest/recommend/insist + that + S + V(原形)
correct_example: I suggest that he attend the meeting.

common_errors.wrong: I suggest that he attends the meeting.
common_errors.why: 仮定法現在→三単現のsは不要
suggest + that節。to不定詞は不可

key_signal: suggest/recommend/insist/demand + that S + 原形

rule: come into effect
rule_en: Come Into Effect (Collocation)
pattern: come into effect/force = 施行される、発効する
correct_example: The new regulation will come into effect next month.

common_errors.why: inではなくinto
toではなくinto

key_signal: come into: effect/force/being/existence/contact
